message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 6
Recent Green phase: Added reproduction logic — gather neighbor cells of live cells as candidates (via 3x3 loop), dedup with a Map, keep ones with exactly 3 live neighbors and not currently alive. Survivors + births returned.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 6 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code for refactoring opportunities.

## Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

- `nextGeneration` — Clear, idiomatic Game of Life terminology. Keep.
- `isNeighbor` — Clear. Keep.
- `countLiveNeighbors` — Clear. Keep.
- `survives` — Clear. Keep.
- `candidates` — Reasonable. These are dead neighbor cells that might be born.
- `births` — Clear.

Names are good overall.

## APP Mass Calculation (Before)

Counting components in `nextGeneration`:
- Constants: `-1`, `1`, `0`, `0`, `0`, `1`, `1`, `0`, `0`, `${...},${...}`, `3` ≈ 11
- Bindings (params, vars, destructures): liveCells, survivors, candidates, x, y, dx, dy, cell, cell[0], cell[1], births, cell, lx, ly = ~14
- Invocations: filter, survives, set, for-of (values), some, countLiveNeighbors, push = ~7 × 2 = 14
- Conditionals: `if (dx === 0 && dy === 0)`, `if (liveCells.some...)`, `if (countLive... === 3)` = 3 × 4 = 12
- Loops: for-of liveCells, for dx, for dy, for-of candidates.values() = 4 × 5 = 20
- Assignments: `candidates.set` could count, `births.push` could count, `=` for cell = mostly bindings

Total approximate mass: high, around 70+

## Refactoring Opportunities

### Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) & Rule 3 (No Duplication)

Main issues I see:

1. **Duplication of "is this point in liveCells"** — There's `isNeighbor`/equality logic and the `.some(([lx, ly]) => lx === cell[0] && ly === cell[1])` membership check. These reuse different patterns.

2. **The reproduction loop reuses ad-hoc string keys** — But the survivors phase doesn't use them. We could unify the candidate-generation approach with a `key` helper.

3. **Iteration over all `liveCells` to count neighbors is O(n²)** — but I won't optimize unless it simplifies. Actually, using a Set lookup simplifies and is clearer.

4. **The dead neighbor candidate logic is wordy** — The 3x3 loop and `dx===0 && dy===0` skip is verbose. We could extract a `neighborsOf` helper that both `countLiveNeighbors` and the candidate generation could use, removing the duplication between `isNeighbor`-based counting and `dx/dy` iteration.

Let me focus on the most impactful refactoring: **extract `neighborsOf(cell)` helper** that returns the 8 surrounding cells. This:
- Removes duplication (we use neighborhood concept twice: once via `isNeighbor`, once via dx/dy loop)
- Reveals intent (clearer than dx/dy nested loops)
- Allows `countLiveNeighbors` to use a Set lookup for live-cell membership

Let me refactor with one cohesive change.

content.text:
All 6 tests still passing. Let me document the refactoring.

## Refactoring Summary

### Naming Evaluation
- All names already reveal intent clearly: `nextGeneration`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `neighborsOf`, `cellKey`. No renames needed.
- Removed `isNeighbor` and `survives` helpers — see below.

### Changes Applied (one cohesive refactoring)

**Extracted `neighborsOf(cell)` helper** that returns all 8 surrounding cells. This:

1. **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The 3x3 dx/dy nested loop with `if (dx === 0 && dy === 0) continue` is replaced by a named `neighborsOf` helper used at the call site. Reading `for (const neighbor of neighborsOf(cell))` immediately reveals intent.

2. **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Previously, "what are the 8 neighbors of a cell" was expressed two different ways — `isNeighbor` (Chebyshev distance check) for counting, and dx/dy iteration for candidate generation. Now both call sites use the same `neighborsOf` helper. The Chebyshev-distance `isNeighbor` was removed entirely as it became unused.

3. **Rule 3 (Conceptual Duplication)**: The membership check `liveCells.some(([lx, ly]) => lx === cell[0] && ly === cell[1])` (linear scan) and the candidates Map (string-key-based dedup) were doing conceptually similar work with two different mechanisms. Unified on a single `liveSet: Set<string>` keyed by `cellKey()`, which is reused for:
   - counting live neighbors (O(1) lookup instead of O(n) `.filter` over `liveCells`)
   - checking whether a candidate is already alive

4. **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: 
   - `isNeighbor` removed (dead code after refactor)
   - `survives` inlined as a small arrow inside the `.filter` — it was only used once and the inlined form is just as readable (`count === 2 || count === 3`).
   - `births` built with `.filter` instead of an imperative `for-of + push`, removing one explicit loop + assignment.

### APP Mass Comparison (approximate)

Before: 
- 1 module-level helper (`isNeighbor`) + 2 helpers + `nextGeneration` with nested 3x3 dx/dy loops, two `if` branches, manual `births.push`, `.some` for membership.
- Estimated mass: ~75 (heavy on nested loops, conditionals, and the imperative births block).

After:
- 1 small `cellKey`, 1 `neighborsOf` (still has the 3x3 loop, but now isolated and reusable), 1 `countLiveNeighbors` (one filter), `nextGeneration` linear top-to-bottom with two `.filter` passes and one nested `for-of`.
- Estimated mass: ~60 (eliminated `isNeighbor`, the `.some` linear scan, the imperative births accumulation, and `survives`; nested 3x3 logic is now confined to one helper).

Mass reduced and — more importantly — Rule 2 (intent) improved: `nextGeneration` now reads as "survivors (live cells with 2 or 3 live neighbors) plus births (dead neighbors with exactly 3 live neighbors)", which matches the spec's language directly.

### Tests
All 6 tests passing — same before and after.

Files modified:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_12-33-27_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
